This file contains the full text content of all pages on kansascitybankruptcymill.com.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City: Dismissal Rates, Costs & What the Data Shows
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcymill.com/chapter-13-kansas-city.html

Skip to main content

kansascitybankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
Discharge Screener
Research

Updated March 2026 - FJC Data Through 2024

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City:
What the Court Data Actually Shows

Thinking about filing Chapter 13 in Kansas City? Before you hire an attorney, look at the numbers. This page presents federal court data on Chapter 13 outcomes in the Western District of Missouri and the District of Kansas - the two courts that serve the KC metro area.

Home›Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City: Dismissal Rates, Costs & What the Data Shows›Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City:What the Court Data Actually Shows

5,056

Ch.13 cases filed
W.D. Mo. (2018-2024)

44.4%

dismissed without
discharge

48.7%

received
a discharge

3-5 yr

plan duration
(typical)

What Is Chapter 13 Bankruptcy?

Chapter 13 is a form of bankruptcy reorganization that allows individuals with regular income to repay some or all of their debts over a 3-to-5-year plan. Unlike Chapter 7, which liquidates non-exempt assets for a quick discharge, Chapter 13 requires monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee who distributes the funds to creditors according to the plan.

Chapter 13 is often used by people who:

Earn too much to qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

Want to save a home from foreclosure by curing mortgage arrears through the plan

Have non-exempt assets they want to keep

Need to repay priority debts like taxes or domestic support obligations

Had a prior bankruptcy that bars them from a Chapter 7 discharge under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f)

The appeal of Chapter 13 is real: it can stop foreclosure, halt wage garnishment, and provide a structured path to becoming debt-free. But the data shows that for many Kansas City filers, the promise of Chapter 13 does not match the outcome.

The Kansas City Chapter 13 Track Record

Between 2018 and 2024, 5,056 Chapter 13 cases were filed in the Western District of Missouri. Of the cases that have reached a final resolution:

44.4% were dismissed - meaning the debtor lost bankruptcy protection, received no discharge, and may have spent months or years making payments for nothing. Only 48.7% resulted in a successful discharge.

Year-by-Year Outcomes - W.D. Mo. Chapter 13

Year FiledTotal FiledDismissedDischargedStill PendingDismiss Rate

2018848266519231.4%

20199353125312433.4%

20206241963395131.4%

202148715113518631.0%

20226262146632734.2%

20237382071549528.0%

2024798123566615.4%

2022-2024 cohorts are mostly still in their 3-5 year plans. Mature cohorts (2018-2020) provide the clearest picture of ultimate outcomes. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

How Kansas Compares

The District of Kansas - covering the Kansas side of the KC metro including Overland Park, Olathe, and Shawnee - tells a different story:

MetricW.D. Mo.D. Kan.Difference

Ch.13 filed (2018-2024)5,0566,114--

Dismissal rate (resolved)44.4%35.5%-8.9 pts

Discharge rate (resolved)48.7%63.8%+15.1 pts

High-volume (500+) dismiss rate42.0%26.2%-15.8 pts

Prior filer rate10.2%----

Same metro, different outcomes. If you live on the Kansas side of the state line, your Chapter 13 case is 15 percentage points more likely to result in a discharge. The courts serve the same economic population. The difference is in attorney practice patterns and court culture.

Why Chapter 13 Cases Fail in Kansas City

Understanding why cases fail is critical to understanding whether your case is likely to succeed. In the Western District of Missouri, dismissed cases break down as follows:

Reason for DismissalShareWhat It Means

Failed plan payments42.0%Debtor could not keep up with monthly payments to the trustee

Other reason36.5%Catch-all category including voluntary dismissal, conversion, and procedural issues

Failed to pay filing fee13.0%Court filing fee ($313) not paid - often an early warning of financial distress

Failed to file information8.4%Required documents (schedules, tax returns, pay stubs) not filed on time

The Plan Payment Problem

Failed plan payments account for 42% of all dismissals. But this number alone does not explain whether the failures are debtor-driven (genuine financial hardship) or attorney-driven (unrealistic plans filed without adequate budgeting).

When an attorney files a Chapter 13 plan with monthly payments the debtor cannot sustain, the case is set up to fail from day one. The debtor makes payments for months - sometimes years - before the plan is dismissed. The attorney, however, has already been paid (or is being paid through the plan). The incentive to ensure plan feasibility is structurally weak in high-volume practices where the attorney may never personally review the debtor's budget.

The Document Filing Problem

8.4% of dismissals occur because required documents were never filed. This is an attorney responsibility. Failure to file schedules, tax returns, or pay stubs is not a client problem - it is a practice management problem. In a well-run office, these deadlines are calendared and tracked. In a mill, they fall through the cracks.

Attorney Market Concentration

The Western District of Missouri has one of the most concentrated Chapter 13 markets in the country:

Volume TierAttorneysCasesMarket ShareDismiss Rate

500+ cases1516,50580.6%42.0%

200-499 cases62,15610.5%41.4%

100-199 cases81,3286.5%43.3%

50-99 cases31940.9%50.0%

Under 50 cases542901.4%62.7%

15 attorneys control 80.6% of the Chapter 13 market. This means a Kansas City debtor's outcome is largely determined by which of these attorneys they hire. The dismissal rates across all volume tiers hover around 41-43%, suggesting systemic issues rather than isolated bad actors.

For comparison, high-volume attorneys in the District of Kansas achieve a 26.2% dismissal rate - 16 points lower than the same category in W.D. Mo. The concentration is not the problem. The outcomes are.

The Chapter 13 Process: What to Expect

If you are considering Chapter 13 in Kansas City, here is what the process looks like:

1

Pre-filing credit counseling

Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from an approved agency. Costs $15-50. Cannot be waived.

2

Filing the petition and plan

Your attorney files the petition, schedules, and proposed Chapter 13 plan with the court. The filing fee is $313. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping most creditor collection activity.

3

The 341 meeting of creditors

Held 21-50 days after filing. The 341 meeting is conducted by the Chapter 13 trustee, who asks questions about your finances and plan. Creditors may attend but rarely do.

4

Plan confirmation

The court holds a confirmation hearing to approve your plan. Creditors may object. If confirmed, you make monthly payments to the trustee for 3-5 years.

5

Monthly payments (3-5 years)

You make payments to the trustee, who distributes funds to creditors. Below-median-income debtors: 3-year plan. Above-median-income: 5 years. Your attorney fees are typically paid through the plan.

6

Discharge (if successful)

After completing all plan payments and a post-filing debtor education course, the court enters a discharge order. Remaining qualifying debts are discharged. In W.D. Mo., only 48.7% of filers reach this step.

Discharge Eligibility: The 1328(f) Bar

Before filing Chapter 13, your attorney should check whether you are even eligible for a discharge. Under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f), the court cannot grant a discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the last 4 years, or

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the last 2 years

In the Western District of Missouri, 10.2% of Chapter 13 filers had a prior bankruptcy filing in the same court. Some of these filers may be legally ineligible for a discharge before their case is even filed. An attorney who does not check this before filing is not performing basic due diligence.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility - Free Tool

Questions to Ask Before Hiring a Chapter 13 Attorney

What is your Chapter 13 dismissal rate? The W.D. Mo. average is 44.4%. Ask for their number. If they don't know it, that is a red flag.

How many active Chapter 13 cases are you currently managing? An attorney with 300+ active cases may not have time for yours.

Will you personally review my budget and plan? In many high-volume firms, paralegals prepare the plan with minimal attorney oversight.

Have you checked my 1328(f) eligibility? If you have a prior bankruptcy, this must be verified before filing.

What happens if my case is dismissed? Understand who keeps the fees and what options you have.

What is your total fee, and how is it paid? Get the full number in writing. Compare to the district fee guidelines.

How do you handle plan modifications? Life changes. Your attorney should be willing to modify the plan rather than let it fail.

Can I reach you directly? Ask who will actually handle your questions. If the answer is "my paralegal," consider whether that is adequate for your situation.

When Chapter 13 Fails: Your Options

If you fall behind on plan payments or your financial circumstances change, your Chapter 13 case is at risk of dismissal. But dismissal is not automatic. You have several options, and understanding them before you need them can save your case.

Plan Modification

Under 11 U.S.C. Section 1329, you or your attorney can ask the court to modify your plan after confirmation. Common modifications include reducing the monthly payment amount, extending the plan duration (up to the 5-year maximum), or adjusting how creditors are paid. If your income has dropped due to job loss, medical issues, or other hardship, a modification may allow you to keep your case alive rather than losing everything you have paid so far. Your attorney should be proactive about proposing modifications when your circumstances change - not waiting until the trustee files a motion to dismiss.

Hardship Discharge

In rare cases, you may qualify for a hardship discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 1328(b), even if you have not completed all plan payments. To qualify, you must show that: (1) your failure to complete payments is due to circumstances beyond your control, (2) unsecured creditors have received at least as much as they would have received in a Chapter 7 liquidation, and (3) modification of the plan is not practicable. Hardship discharges are uncommon but can provide relief if you develop a serious disability, lose your primary income source permanently, or face other genuine hardship.

Conversion to Chapter 7

If your Chapter 13 plan is failing and you now qualify for Chapter 7, conversion may be your best option. Under 11 U.S.C. Section 1307(a), you have the right to convert your case to Chapter 7 at any time, provided the case was not previously converted from Chapter 7. Conversion gives you a fresh start with a Chapter 7 discharge in 3-4 months instead of continuing a plan you cannot complete. However, property that was protected by the Chapter 13 plan may be subject to liquidation in Chapter 7, so this decision requires careful analysis of your current exemptions.

Voluntary Dismissal

You can ask to dismiss your own Chapter 13 case. After dismissal, you lose the protection of the automatic stay, creditors can resume collection, and any mortgage arrears cured through the plan may become due again. If you refile Chapter 13 within one year, the automatic stay may be limited to 30 days under 11 U.S.C. Section 362(c)(3), or may not take effect at all if you had two or more prior cases dismissed in the past year.

The Cost of a Failed Chapter 13

When a Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the debtor does not just lose the bankruptcy protection - they may lose years of payments with nothing to show for it. Understanding the true cost of failure is essential before deciding to file.

Attorney Fees in Dismissed Cases

In most Kansas City Chapter 13 cases, attorney fees are paid through the plan before unsecured creditors receive anything. This means your attorney may receive their full fee - or a substantial portion of it - even if your case is dismissed and you receive no discharge. The court approves these fees under 11 U.S.C. Section 330, and they are deducted from your plan payments. If your case fails after 2 years of payments, your attorney may have been paid $3,000-5,000 while you walk away with no debt relief.

Lost Time and Compounding Debt

A dismissed Chapter 13 case means 1-3 years of plan payments that did not result in a discharge. During that time, nondischargeable debts like student loans and recent taxes continued to accrue interest. If the case is dismissed, creditors who were not paid in full through the plan can resume collection for the full remaining balance - plus any interest and fees that accumulated while the case was pending. For some debtors, their financial situation is worse after a failed Chapter 13 than it was before filing.

Impact on Future Filings

A dismissed case affects your ability to refile. If you refile within 180 days of dismissal, the automatic stay may be limited or eliminated entirely. Under Section 109(g), you may be barred from refiling for 180 days if the case was dismissed for willful failure to comply with court orders. Serial filings also draw increased scrutiny from the court and the U.S. Trustee.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 success rate in Kansas City?

In the Western District of Missouri, only 48.7% of resolved Chapter 13 cases result in a discharge. The District of Kansas performs better at 63.8%. A Chapter 13 "success" means completing all plan payments over 3-5 years and receiving a discharge of remaining qualifying debts.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Kansas City?

Chapter 13 attorney fees in Kansas City typically range from $3,000 to $5,000, paid through the bankruptcy plan over 3-5 years. The court filing fee is $313. Total out-of-pocket depends on your plan terms. Importantly, attorney fees are often paid even when the case is dismissed - meaning the attorney earns their fee regardless of whether you receive a discharge.

Why are so many Chapter 13 cases dismissed in Kansas City?

42% of dismissals in W.D. Mo. are due to failed plan payments. Other causes include failure to file required documents (8.4%) and failure to pay the filing fee (13%). The high dismissal rate may also reflect attorney practices: when 80.6% of cases are handled by just 15 attorneys, individual attention can suffer.

How long does Chapter 13 take in Kansas City?

A Chapter 13 plan runs 3 to 5 years. Below-median-income debtors may qualify for a 3-year plan; above-median-income debtors must commit to 5 years. If the case is dismissed before completion, the debtor receives no discharge and may have spent years making payments with no debt relief.

Can I switch from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7?

Yes, conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 is generally available under 11 U.S.C. § 1307(a) as a matter of right, provided the case was not previously converted from Chapter 7. This can be an important option if your circumstances change. However, Chapter 7 has its own eligibility requirements including the means test.

What happens to my house in Chapter 13?

Chapter 13 can stop foreclosure and allow you to cure mortgage arrears through the plan. As long as you make your plan payments and stay current on ongoing mortgage payments, you can keep your home. However, if the case is dismissed, the foreclosure protection ends and the lender can resume collection.

Should I file Chapter 13 or Chapter 7?

The choice depends on your income, assets, and goals. Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months to discharge) and works well for people with limited income and few non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is better if you need to save a home, repay priority debts, or don't qualify for Chapter 7. See our Chapter 7 guide for a detailed comparison.

About This Data

Statistics on this page are derived from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, covering 5,056 Chapter 13 cases filed in the Western District of Missouri and 6,114 in the District of Kansas between 2018 and 2024. Attorney volume and concentration data covers the full database period.

This site does not identify individual attorneys by name. This is an educational resource, not legal advice.

Cited in Federal Rules Suggestion 26-BK-3

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City: Costs, Timeline & Discharge Data (2026)
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcymill.com/chapter-7-kansas-city.html

Skip to main content

kansascitybankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
Discharge Screener
Research

93%+ Discharge Rate - W.D. Mo. Data Through 2024

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City:
Costs, Timeline & What the Data Shows

Chapter 7 is the most common form of bankruptcy and has the highest success rate. In the Western District of Missouri, over 93% of Chapter 7 filers receive a discharge. Here is everything the court data tells us about filing Chapter 7 in Kansas City.

Home›Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City: Costs, Timeline & Discharge Data (2026)›Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City:Costs, Timeline & What the Data Shows

3,135

Ch.7 cases filed
W.D. Mo. (2018-2024)

93.1%

discharge rate
(2024 cohort)

<1%

dismissal
rate

3-4 mo

typical timeline
to discharge

The good news: Chapter 7 works. Unlike Chapter 13, where 44.4% of W.D. Mo. cases are dismissed, Chapter 7 has a consistent 93%+ discharge rate. The risk of failure is low - but choosing the right attorney still matters for exemption planning, reaffirmation decisions, and avoiding unnecessary complications.

What Is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Chapter 7 is a liquidation bankruptcy. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets, sells any non-exempt property to pay creditors, and remaining qualifying debts are discharged - typically within 3 to 4 months of filing. In practice, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases, meaning the debtor has no non-exempt property for the trustee to liquidate.

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts including:

Credit card balances

Medical bills

Personal loans

Past-due utility bills

Some older tax debts

Deficiency balances from repossessions or foreclosures

Chapter 7 does not discharge:

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases under 11 U.S.C. § 523(a)(8))

Recent tax debts (generally less than 3 years old)

Domestic support obligations (child support, alimony)

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Most government fines and penalties

Chapter 7 Outcomes in Kansas City

Year-by-Year Data - W.D. Mo.

Year FiledTotal FiledDismissedDischargedDismiss RateDischarge Rate

201868856440.7%93.6%

201962275771.1%92.8%

202049634680.6%94.4%

202138243651.0%95.5%

202225522430.8%95.3%

202330152731.7%90.7%

202439123640.5%93.1%

Source: FJC Integrated Database. 2023-2024 may have some cases still pending.

The difference from Chapter 13 is notable. Chapter 7 discharge rates have stayed above 90% every year since 2018, while Chapter 13 dismissal rates run 31-34% in the same court. If you qualify for Chapter 7 and don't need Chapter 13's specific protections (mortgage cure, priority debt repayment), the data strongly favors Chapter 7.

Filing Volume Trend

Chapter 7 filings in W.D. Mo. dropped 63% from 2018 to 2022 (688 to 255), largely due to COVID-era stimulus payments and enhanced unemployment benefits. Filings have rebounded since, reaching 391 in 2024 - 57% of pre-COVID levels and climbing.

MetricW.D. Mo.D. Kan.

Ch.7 cases filed (2018-2024)3,135--

Discharge rate (2024)93.1%~95%

Dismissal rate<1%<1%

Ch.7 as share of all filings38.3%36.7%

How Much Does Chapter 7 Cost in Kansas City?

Court Filing Fee

$338

Set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments or waived for low-income filers (Form 103B).

Attorney Fees

$1,000 - $2,500

Typical range in Kansas City. Must be paid upfront (before filing). Compare to court fee guidelines for your district.

Credit Counseling

$15 - $50

Two required courses: pre-filing counseling and post-filing debtor education. Online options available.

Total Typical Cost

$1,400 - $2,900

All-in estimate including filing fee, attorney, and both counseling courses.

Important: Unlike Chapter 13, where attorney fees can be paid through the plan, Chapter 7 fees are typically due before filing. Be wary of any attorney who asks you to charge the legal fees to a credit card - this can create a nondischargeable debt if done shortly before filing.

Do You Qualify? The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test under 11 U.S.C. § 707(b). The test has two parts:

Step 1: Income Comparison

Compare your household's current monthly income (averaged over the 6 months before filing) to the state median for your family size. If you're below median, you pass. Current median income thresholds for Kansas City area filers:

Family SizeMissouri MedianKansas Median

1 person$58,854$60,477

2 people$73,993$78,359

3 people$84,476$89,485

4 people$101,566$104,463

Figures approximate and updated periodically by the U.S. Trustee Program. Verify current figures at meanstest.org.

Step 2: Disposable Income Calculation

If your income exceeds the median, a detailed calculation subtracts allowed expenses (IRS standards plus actual secured debt payments) from your income. If your remaining disposable income is too low to fund a meaningful Chapter 13 plan, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Check Your Means Test Eligibility

The Chapter 7 Timeline

1

Pre-filing credit counseling

Required within 180 days before filing. Approved agencies available online ($15-50). Takes about 1 hour.

2

Filing the petition

Your attorney files the petition, schedules, means test, and Statement of Financial Affairs. The automatic stay takes effect immediately. Filing fee: $338.

3

341 meeting of creditors (Day 21-50)

The 341 meeting is conducted by the Chapter 7 trustee. Typically lasts 5-15 minutes. You answer questions about your finances under oath. Creditors may attend but rarely do in consumer cases.

4

Objection deadline (Day 60 after 341)

Creditors and the trustee have 60 days after the 341 meeting to object to discharge or file a complaint under section 523. Most cases see no objections.

5

Post-filing debtor education

Second required course, completed after filing but before discharge. Online, $15-50, about 2 hours.

6

Discharge (Day ~90-120)

The court enters the discharge order. Qualifying debts are eliminated. 93%+ of Kansas City filers reach this step. The entire process typically takes 3 to 4 months.

Discharge Eligibility: The 727(a)(8) and 727(a)(9) Bars

Even if you pass the means test, you may be ineligible for a Chapter 7 discharge if you've filed bankruptcy before. Under 11 U.S.C. § 727(a)(8) and (a)(9):

Prior Chapter 7 discharge: Must wait 8 years before receiving another Chapter 7 discharge

Prior Chapter 13 discharge: Must wait 6 years (unless the prior Chapter 13 paid 100% of unsecured claims or 70% under a good-faith plan)

In W.D. Mo., 3.3% of Chapter 7 filers had a prior bankruptcy filing. If your attorney does not check these eligibility bars before filing, you could go through the entire process and be denied a discharge at the end.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility - Free Tool

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

FactorChapter 7Chapter 13

Timeline3-4 months3-5 years

Success rate (W.D. Mo.)93%+48.7%

Monthly paymentsNoneRequired (3-5 years)

Keep your homeOnly if current + exemptCan cure arrears through plan

Income limitMust pass means testNo upper limit

Attorney fees$1,000-$2,500 (upfront)$3,000-$5,000 (through plan)

Repeat filing wait8 years (Ch.7-to-Ch.7)2 years (Ch.13-to-Ch.13)

Non-exempt assetsMay be liquidatedProtected

The data speaks clearly: If you qualify for Chapter 7 and don't need to save a home from foreclosure or repay priority debts, Chapter 7 is faster, cheaper, and dramatically more likely to succeed. The 93% vs. 48.7% discharge rate gap should be part of every Kansas City debtor's decision-making process.

Kansas and Missouri Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in Chapter 7. Missouri and Kansas have different exemption systems:

Missouri Exemptions (Key Highlights)

Homestead: $15,000 ($30,000 for married couples filing jointly)

Vehicle: $3,000 per vehicle

Personal property: $3,000 aggregate

Wildcard: $600 plus up to $3,500 of unused homestead

Retirement accounts: Generally fully exempt (ERISA-qualified plans)

Kansas Exemptions (Key Highlights)

Homestead: Unlimited value, up to 1 acre in city / 160 acres rural

Vehicle: $20,000 (one vehicle)

Personal property: Various categories with specific limits

Retirement accounts: Generally fully exempt

Kansas has a stronger homestead exemption (unlimited value) and a significantly higher vehicle exemption ($20,000 vs. $3,000). If you live near the state line, which exemption system applies to you depends on where you have lived for the majority of the 730 days before filing. Consult an attorney about the domicile rules.

What to Know Before Choosing an Attorney

Chapter 7 has a high success rate in Kansas City, but the quality of your experience still depends on the attorney you hire. Here are the factors that matter most:

Exemption Planning

Your attorney's most important job in Chapter 7 is making sure your property is protected. Missouri and Kansas have very different exemption systems, and the rules about which state's exemptions apply depend on where you have lived for the past two years. An attorney who does not carefully analyze your exemptions before filing could expose property that should have been protected - or miss opportunities to maximize your protections.

Reaffirmation Decisions

If you have a car loan or other secured debt you want to keep paying, your attorney should explain the pros and cons of reaffirmation versus redemption versus surrender. Reaffirming a debt means you remain personally liable even after bankruptcy. In some cases, continuing to make payments without reaffirming - known as "ride-through" - may be an option, though not all lenders cooperate. An attorney who pressures you to reaffirm without explaining the risks is not looking out for your interests.

Fee Transparency

Chapter 7 fees in Kansas City range from $1,000 to $2,500. Be cautious of any attorney who quotes a low fee upfront but adds charges later for "additional schedules," "asset review," or "creditor negotiations." Ask for the total cost in writing before you sign a retainer agreement. The court requires attorneys to disclose their fees on a public filing (Rule 2016(b)), so your attorney's fees will be a matter of record.

Volume and Attention

Some Kansas City bankruptcy attorneys handle hundreds of cases per year. High volume is not inherently bad - the 93%+ discharge rate reflects the work of high-volume filers. But you should ask whether the attorney will personally review your petition and attend your 341 meeting, or whether those tasks will be handled by staff. You deserve to know who is actually doing the work on your case.

Life After Chapter 7 Discharge

Getting your discharge is not the end of the process - it is the beginning of rebuilding. Here is what to expect in the months and years after your Chapter 7 case closes.

Credit Rebuilding Timeline

Most Chapter 7 filers can open a secured credit card within 1-3 months of discharge. Secured cards require a refundable deposit (typically $200-500) and report to all three credit bureaus. By using the card for small purchases and paying the balance in full each month, most people see meaningful credit score improvement within 12-18 months. Many filers reach credit scores of 650-700 within 2-3 years of discharge.

Debts That Survive

Your discharge eliminates most unsecured debts, but some obligations survive. Student loans, recent tax debts, child support, alimony, and debts incurred through fraud are generally nondischargeable under Section 523. Review your discharge order carefully to understand which debts were eliminated and which remain. If a creditor continues to collect on a discharged debt, that may violate the discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. Section 524.

Future Borrowing

Despite the common fear that bankruptcy "ruins your credit forever," most people find that lenders are willing to extend credit sooner than expected. Auto loans are often available within 6-12 months of discharge, though at higher interest rates initially. FHA mortgage loans may be available as soon as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge, and conventional mortgages typically require a 4-year waiting period. Each year of responsible credit use after discharge improves your terms.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Kansas City?

The court filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $2,500, paid before filing. Credit counseling courses cost $15-50 each (two required). Total typical cost: $1,400 to $2,900.

What is the Chapter 7 discharge rate in Kansas City?

In W.D. Mo., 93.1% of Chapter 7 cases filed in 2024 resulted in a discharge. The rate has stayed above 90% consistently since 2018. Less than 1% of cases are dismissed.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

Typically 3 to 4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting is held 21-50 days after filing, and discharge enters about 60 days after that.

Will I lose my car?

In Missouri, you can exempt up to $3,000 per vehicle. In Kansas, the vehicle exemption is $20,000. If your car's equity (value minus loan balance) is within the exemption, you keep it. If you're making payments, you can typically keep the car by staying current or reaffirming the debt.

Will I lose my house?

If your home equity is within the exemption amount ($15,000 in Missouri, unlimited in Kansas) and you're current on mortgage payments, you can keep your home. If you're behind on payments, Chapter 13 may be a better option as it allows you to cure arrears through the plan.

Can I file Chapter 7 again after a previous bankruptcy?

You can file at any time, but discharge is barred for 8 years after a prior Chapter 7 discharge and 6 years after a Chapter 13 discharge (with limited exceptions). Use the free eligibility screener to check your dates.

Do I need an attorney for Chapter 7?

You can file pro se (without an attorney), but it is generally not recommended for most filers. Exemption planning, means test calculations, and reaffirmation decisions involve legal judgment. The 93%+ discharge rate assumes competent representation. See prosedebtors.org if you're considering self-representation.

How will Chapter 7 affect my credit?

A Chapter 7 filing stays on your credit report for 10 years. However, most filers see their credit scores begin recovering within 12-18 months after discharge, often reaching the mid-600s within 2-3 years. The discharge eliminates the debts that were damaging your score.

About This Data

Statistics on this page are derived from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, covering 3,135 Chapter 7 cases filed in the Western District of Missouri between 2018 and 2024. Median income figures are approximate and should be verified against current U.S. Trustee Program data.

This is an educational resource, not legal advice.

Cited in Federal Rules Suggestion 26-BK-3

## Kansas City Bankruptcy Mill: What the Court Data Shows
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcymill.com/

Skip to main content

kansascitybankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
Discharge Screener
Research

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project - 161+ free domains, 115,000+ pages, $0 cost

Federal Court Data - W.D. Mo. & D. Kan.

Kansas City Bankruptcy Mill:
What the Court Data Actually Shows

An independent, data-driven analysis of bankruptcy outcomes in the Kansas City metropolitan area. Based on 5,056 Chapter 13 cases filed in the Western District of Missouri and 6,114 in the District of Kansas between 2018 and 2024.

44.4%

W.D. Mo. Ch.13
dismissal rate

35.5%

D. Kan. Ch.13
dismissal rate

80.6%

of W.D. Mo. Ch.13 cases
filed by just 15 attorneys

Two courts. Same metro area. Very different outcomes. A Chapter 13 debtor in the Western District of Missouri is 25% more likely to have their case dismissed than a debtor across the state line in the District of Kansas. Both courts serve the Kansas City metro. The difference is not geography - it is practice.

The Kansas City Numbers

The Kansas City metropolitan area is split between two federal bankruptcy courts: the Western District of Missouri (W.D. Mo., covering the Missouri side) and the District of Kansas (D. Kan., covering the Kansas side). Both serve the same economic population. But their Chapter 13 outcomes diverge sharply.

Chapter 13 Outcomes by Year - W.D. Mo.

YearFiledDismissedDischargedDismiss RateDischarge Rate

201884826651931.4%61.2%

201993531253133.4%56.8%

202062419633931.4%54.3%

202148715113531.0%27.7%

20226262146634.2%10.5%

20237382071528.0%2.0%

2024798123515.4%0.6%

Note: 2022-2024 cases are mostly still pending. Mature cohorts (2018-2020) show the clearest picture. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Chapter 13 Outcomes by Year - D. Kan.

YearFiledDismissedDischargedDismiss RateDischarge Rate

20181,13427086123.8%75.9%

20191,20830887225.5%72.2%

202070817647624.9%67.2%

202163118023928.5%37.9%

202266517512226.3%18.3%

20238521831321.5%1.5%

2024916145215.8%0.2%

Head-to-Head: W.D. Mo. vs. D. Kan.

Ch.13 dismissal rate (resolved cases, 2018-2024)
44.4%
vs
35.5%

Ch.13 discharge rate (resolved cases)
48.7%
vs
63.8%

High-volume attorney dismissal rate (500+ cases)
42.0%
vs
26.2%

Market concentration (top 15 attorneys)
80.6%
vs
--

Prior filer rate (Ch.13)
10.2%
vs
--

Market Concentration

The Kansas City Missouri-side bankruptcy market is one of the most concentrated in the country. A small number of attorneys handle the vast majority of cases - a structural condition that can enable mill-like practices when combined with poor outcomes.

Attorney VolumeAttorneysCh.13 CasesShare of All FilingsDismissal Rate

500+ cases1516,50580.6%42.0%

200-499 cases62,15610.5%41.4%

100-199 cases81,3286.5%43.3%

50-99 cases31940.9%50.0%

Under 50 cases542901.4%62.7%

What this means: 21 attorneys filing 200 or more Chapter 13 cases account for 91.1% of all filings in the Western District of Missouri. Whether a debtor gets adequate representation depends almost entirely on which of these attorneys they hire. And the dismissal rates across all tiers - 41% to 43% - suggest systemic problems that go beyond any single firm.

Why Concentration Matters

High market concentration is not inherently bad. An experienced attorney handling many cases can develop expertise and efficiency that benefits clients. The problem arises when volume outpaces capacity - when an attorney takes on more cases than they can adequately serve.

The distinguishing factor is outcomes. In the District of Kansas, high-volume attorneys (500+ cases) have a 26.2% dismissal rate. In the Western District of Missouri, that same category has a 42.0% dismissal rate - 16 percentage points higher. Same metro area, same economic conditions, same types of debtors. The gap is in the practice.

Why Cases Fail in Kansas City

When a Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the debtor loses their bankruptcy protection, may face resumed creditor collection activity, and has spent months or years making payments with nothing to show for it. In the Western District of Missouri, the primary causes of dismissal are:

ReasonShare of Dismissals

Failed plan payments42.0%

Other reason36.5%

Failed to pay filing fee13.0%

Failed to file required information8.4%

"Failed plan payments" is the most common reason, but this category masks a more nuanced reality. Plan failures can result from genuinely changed circumstances - job loss, medical emergencies, divorce. But they can also result from poorly designed plans that were never realistic, based on income and expense calculations that did not reflect the debtor's actual situation.

The "filed to fail" pattern: When an attorney files a Chapter 13 plan that is unlikely to succeed, the debtor still pays attorney fees (often through the plan itself), the attorney still earns their fee, and the client bears the full cost of failure. This creates a perverse incentive: the attorney profits regardless of outcome. Identifying this pattern requires comparing individual attorney dismissal rates against the court average.

Prior Filers

One in ten Chapter 13 filers in the Western District of Missouri (10.2%) had a prior bankruptcy filing in the same court. Prior filers face additional legal barriers under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f), which can bar them from receiving a discharge entirely. An attorney who files a case for a client who is legally ineligible for discharge is, at minimum, not performing adequate due diligence.

What To Look For

If you are considering hiring a bankruptcy attorney in the Kansas City area - or if you are already represented and wondering whether your case is being handled adequately - here are data-backed questions to ask:

1. What is your dismissal rate?

Any attorney should be able to tell you what percentage of their Chapter 13 cases are dismissed. If they don't know or won't say, that is itself a signal. The W.D. Mo. average is 44.4%. An attorney significantly above that deserves scrutiny.

2. How many active cases do you personally handle?

There is no magic number, but an attorney handling 200+ active Chapter 13 cases while also maintaining new case intake may not have the bandwidth for meaningful individual attention. Ask who will actually work on your case day-to-day.

3. Will you file my Monthly Operating Reports on time?

In Chapter 11 and some Chapter 13 cases, Monthly Operating Reports (MORs) are required. Chronic failure to file them is a red flag for attorney disengagement. Ask whether your attorney has any cases with delinquent MOR filings.

4. Have you checked whether I'm eligible for a discharge?

Under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f), prior filers face waiting periods before they can receive a discharge. This is a threshold question that should be answered before any filing. Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check yourself.

5. How are your fees structured?

In Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically paid through the bankruptcy plan - meaning you may not realize the full cost until years into your case. Ask for the total fee upfront, and compare it to the court-approved fee guidelines for your district.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a bankruptcy mill?

A bankruptcy mill is a high-volume law firm that processes large numbers of bankruptcy cases with minimal individual attention. These firms often have dismissal rates significantly above the court average, delegate most work to non-attorney staff, and profit regardless of whether the client's case succeeds. The term has no formal legal definition, but the pattern is well-documented in federal court data and recognized by judges, trustees, and bar associations.

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Kansas City?

In the Western District of Missouri (which covers the Missouri side of Kansas City), 44.4% of Chapter 13 cases filed between 2018 and 2024 were dismissed without the debtor receiving a discharge. The neighboring District of Kansas, covering the Kansas side of the metro area, has a dismissal rate of 35.5% - nearly 9 percentage points lower. Both courts serve the same metropolitan area.

How concentrated is the Kansas City bankruptcy market?

Extremely concentrated. Just 15 attorneys handle 80.6% of all Chapter 13 filings in the Western District of Missouri. These 500+ case attorneys have a 42% dismissal rate. By comparison, high-volume attorneys in the District of Kansas have a 26.2% dismissal rate - 16 percentage points lower.

Is a high-volume attorney automatically a bad choice?

No. Volume alone does not make a mill. Some high-volume attorneys achieve excellent outcomes through efficient systems and experienced support staff. The key metric is outcomes: what percentage of their cases result in a successful discharge versus dismissal. A high-volume attorney with a low dismissal rate may actually be a better choice than a low-volume attorney without the infrastructure to manage cases effectively.

How do I check my attorney's track record?

Public court records through PACER contain the outcome of every bankruptcy case. You can search by attorney name to see their full case history, including how many cases were dismissed versus discharged. The 1328f.com discharge screener also provides district-level data on outcomes. Additionally, your state bar association can tell you whether an attorney has any disciplinary history.

What should I do if I think my attorney is a mill?

If you believe your attorney is not providing adequate representation: (1) document specific failures - missed deadlines, unanswered communications, filing errors; (2) request a copy of your complete case file; (3) consult with another bankruptcy attorney for a second opinion; (4) file a complaint with your state bar association if warranted; (5) consider filing a motion to substitute counsel with the court. You have the right to competent representation under the Rules of Professional Conduct.

Can I file on the Kansas side instead of the Missouri side?

Bankruptcy venue is determined by where you have lived for the majority of the 180 days before filing. If you live in Kansas, your case will be filed in the District of Kansas. If you live in Missouri (including Kansas City, MO, Independence, Lee's Summit, etc.), your case will be filed in the Western District of Missouri. You cannot choose between districts based on preference.

About This Data

All statistics on this site are derived from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, a public dataset maintained by the research arm of the federal judiciary. Case-level data is supplemented by public PACER docket records where noted.

This site does not identify individual attorneys by name. The purpose is to present aggregate data that helps Kansas City area residents make informed decisions about bankruptcy representation. Dismissal rates and volume tiers reflect patterns across the court as a whole.

This is an educational resource. It is not legal advice and should not be treated as a substitute for consultation with a qualified attorney.

Cited in Federal Rules Suggestion 26-BK-3

The research methodology behind this data has been submitted to and accepted by the Advisory Committee on Bankruptcy Rules of the Judicial Conference of the United States as Rules Suggestion 26-BK-3, currently pending consideration for amendments to Federal Rule of Bankruptcy Procedure 4004.

## Privacy Policy - kansascitybankruptcymill.com
Source: https://kansascitybankruptcymill.com/privacy.html

kansascitybankruptcymill.com

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending). The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.
